tables.title: Vasoactive Medications in Pediatric Shock
rows: First-line for fluid-refractory septic shock (2025: either epi or norepi acceptable); cardiogenic shock; anaphylaxis (IM 0.01 mg/kg)
First-line for fluid-refractory septic shock (2025: either epi or norepi acceptable); may have mortality benefit in non-cardiac shock
No longer first-line; consider as second-line or adjunct. More arrhythmias than epi/norepi
Adjunct for vasodilatory shock refractory to catecholamine vasopressors; dose-sparing effect
Load: 50 mcg/kg over 10–60 min; Maintenance: 0.25–0.75 mcg/kg/min
PDE3 inhibitor — inodilatation (increased contractility + reduced afterload)
Cardiogenic shock, post-arrest myocardial dysfunction, right heart failure

tables.title: Fluid Resuscitation by Shock Type
rows: Aggressive: 10–20 mL/kg boluses, may tolerate up to 60 mL/kg or more
Monitor for fluid overload if hemorrhagic cause is not controlled
Blood products for hemorrhagic shock; crystalloid for dehydration
Aggressive: 10–20 mL/kg boluses; typical max 40–60 mL/kg before vasoactive support
Reassess after each bolus; transition to vasoactive at 40 mL/kg if no response
Antibiotics within 60 min; vasoactive support when fluid-refractory
10–20 mL/kg bolus; may need more in severe cases
Give IM epinephrine first — fluids alone are insufficient
IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg adult) is the priority treatment
Cautious: 5–10 mL/kg with reassessment; avoid large boluses
Excess volume worsens pulmonary edema; transition to inotropic support early
Milrinone or epinephrine for inotropy; avoid pure vasopressors initially
10–20 mL/kg as temporizing measure while relieving obstruction
Fluids alone will not resolve the problem — relieve the obstruction

tables.title: Monitoring Goals in Pediatric Shock Resuscitation
rows: Reflects adequacy of oxygen delivery relative to consumption; below 70% suggests inadequate cardiac output or high consumption
Elevated or rising lactate indicates ongoing tissue hypoperfusion and anaerobic metabolism
After each bolus and every 15–30 min
Prolonged CRT reflects vasoconstriction and poor peripheral perfusion
Persistent tachycardia despite treatment suggests ongoing shock
Hypotension is a late, ominous sign; treat perfusion before BP drops
Decreased UOP indicates inadequate renal perfusion; a marker of end-organ function
Improving mental status is one of the best indicators of effective resuscitation
